section.title: The Demand for a Sign
section.sectionRationale: The Pharisees and Sadducees, usually rivals, unite to test Jesus by demanding a sign from heaven. He refuses all but the sign of Jonah, and then warns His slow disciples to beware the leaven, the corrupting teaching, of these leaders.
section.thesis: A hardened generation that reads the sky but not the times will be given no sign but the sign of Jonah, the death and resurrection of the Son; and His disciples must guard their hearts against the pervasive leaven of false teaching.
section.frameNote: Two beats: the demand for a sign and the answer of Jonah; and the warning about the leaven of the Pharisees and Sadducees, which the disciples slowly come to understand.

verses.text: Then the Pharisees and Sadducees came and tested Jesus by asking Him to show them a {{semeion|sign}} from heaven.
But He replied, “When evening comes, you say, ‘The weather will be fair, for the sky is red,’
and in the morning, ‘Today it will be stormy, for the sky is red and overcast.’ You know how to interpret the appearance of the sky, but not the signs of the times.
A wicked and adulterous generation demands a sign, but none will be given it except the sign of Jonah.” Then He left them and went away.

units.label: The leaven of the Pharisees and Sadducees

verses.text: When they crossed to the other side, the disciples forgot to take bread.
“Watch out!” Jesus told them. “Beware of the {{zyme|leaven}} of the Pharisees and Sadducees.”
They discussed this among themselves and concluded, “It is because we did not bring any bread.”
Aware of their conversation, Jesus said, “You of {{oligopistos|little faith}}, why are you debating among yourselves about having no bread?
Do you still not understand? Do you not remember the five loaves for the five thousand, and how many basketfuls you gathered?
Or the seven loaves for the four thousand, and how many basketfuls you gathered?
How do you not understand that I was not telling you about bread? But beware of the leaven of the Pharisees and Sadducees.”
Then they understood that He was not telling them to beware of the leaven used in bread, but of the teaching of the Pharisees and Sadducees.

semeion.gloss: A sign, a miraculous token pointing beyond itself. The leaders demand a sign from heaven to compel belief, but a heart that will not read the works already done will not be won by more; the only sign given will be the sign of Jonah.

zyme.gloss: Leaven, yeast, which works invisibly to permeate a whole batch of dough. Jesus uses it as a picture of teaching: the false doctrine of the leaders spreads quietly and corrupts the whole, so it must be watched against.

oligopistos.gloss: Of little faith. A gentle rebuke Jesus reserves for His own disciples, who trust Him truly but too little, forgetting the bread He has already multiplied and fretting over what they lack.

basic.context: The Pharisees and Sadducees, opposing parties who agreed on little, join to test Jesus, demanding a sign from heaven. He refuses: this generation can forecast the weather but cannot read the signs of the times, and the only sign it will get is the sign of Jonah. Crossing the lake, the disciples worry they forgot bread. Jesus warns them to beware the leaven of the Pharisees and Sadducees, and they finally grasp He means their teaching.
basic.hermeneutics: The demand for a sign is not honest seeking but a test meant to trap. The sign of Jonah, three days and nights, points to the resurrection. The leaven is a metaphor for doctrine that spreads invisibly; the two scenes belong together, both about hearts and minds that must discern truth from counterfeit.
basic.originalLanguages: The leaders want a semeion, a compelling sign, but signs do not persuade a hardened heart. Jesus warns of the zyme, the leaven, of false teaching that permeates like yeast. And He calls the anxious disciples oligopistoi, people of little faith, who forget the bread He has already provided.
basic.typology: The sign of Jonah, three days in the belly of the great fish, prefigures Jesus three days in the grave and His rising. Jonah's preaching to Nineveh also foreshadows a message that reaches beyond Israel.
basic.christ: Jesus is the greater Jonah, whose death and resurrection is the definitive sign to the world. He sees through the test, refuses to perform on demand, and points forward to the one sign that matters, the empty tomb.
basic.god: God does not stoop to satisfy hard-hearted curiosity, yet He gives the greatest sign of all in the resurrection. He calls His people to discern the times and to guard against teaching that quietly corrupts.
basic.crossReferences: Jonah 1 and 2 give the sign of the three days. Matthew 12 records an earlier demand and the same answer. 1 Corinthians 5 speaks of a little leaven working through the whole lump. Luke 12 warns of the leaven of the Pharisees, which is hypocrisy.
basic.oneStory: As opposition hardens, Jesus refuses to prove Himself to those who will not believe and points to the one sign that will settle everything: His resurrection, the sign of Jonah. The story presses on toward the cross and the empty tomb, the sign for every generation, while His people learn to beware the leaven that would corrupt their faith.

context.literary: This scene stands between two feedings and Peter's confession, and it echoes the earlier sign demand of chapter 12. Matthew unites the Pharisees and Sadducees, ordinarily opposed, to show the breadth of the rejection. The leaven warning turns on the disciples' obtuseness, a recurring motif, and it prepares the reader to hear Peter's confession in verse 16 as a God-given exception to the surrounding blindness.
context.historical: The Pharisees prized the oral tradition and the resurrection; the Sadducees, a priestly aristocracy, rejected both and denied the resurrection. Their alliance against Jesus was remarkable. Weather lore from the color of the sky was common wisdom. Leaven, a piece of fermented dough, was a familiar image for a small, hidden influence spreading through the whole.
context.canonical: The sign of Jonah ties to Jonah 1:17 and to Matthew 12:39-40, where Jesus makes the three days explicit. The leaven imagery recurs in 1 Corinthians 5:6-8 and Galatians 5:9. The refusal to give a compelling sign accords with the biblical pattern that faith comes by the word, not by spectacle, John 20:29.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show Jesus refusing to authenticate Himself on demand to hardened opponents, pointing instead to His resurrection as the decisive sign, and warning His disciples to discern and resist corrupting teaching.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The refusal describes Jesus' response to a specific test; it prescribes the abiding truth that God is not obligated to satisfy unbelieving demands, and that believers must guard against false doctrine. The rebuke of little faith warns against the anxiety that forgets God's proven provision.

debates.question: What exactly is the sign of Jonah?

views.label: The resurrection after three days
views.case: In Matthew 12:40 Jesus links Jonah's three days in the fish to the Son of Man's three days in the earth, so the sign is chiefly His death and rising, the one vindicating miracle.

views.label: The preaching that summons to repentance
views.case: Jonah's word brought Nineveh to repentance; the sign may also be the proclamation that confronts and converts, so that the true miracle is a message believed rather than a spectacle seen.

debates.bearing: Both cohere: the resurrection is the decisive sign, and the preaching of the crucified and risen Christ is the means by which that sign calls the world to repent.

study.originalLanguages: The leaders demand a semeion, a sign from heaven that would compel belief, but Jesus knows that a heart set against Him will not be persuaded by wonders; the only sign given is the sign of Jonah, His resurrection. He then warns of the zyme, the leaven, the hidden teaching of the Pharisees and Sadducees that works through a life and a community like yeast through dough. When the disciples miss His meaning and fret about literal bread, He calls them oligopistoi, of little faith, gently rebuking those who have seen Him feed thousands yet still forget. Discernment, not spectacle, is what He asks of them.

typology.type: Jonah's three days in the great fish
typology.fulfillment: The Son of Man three days in the heart of the earth and His resurrection, Matthew 12:40, Jonah 1:17.
The gospel proclaimed to the nations, a greater than Jonah calling all to repent, Matthew 12:41.
False teaching, and elsewhere sin, spreading through a community, a warning fulfilled in the church's call to purity, 1 Corinthians 5:6-8.

study.christ: Jesus is the greater than Jonah. He will not be reduced to a wonder-worker on demand, for the faith He seeks is not coerced by spectacle but awakened by the truth. He points beyond every lesser sign to the one that vindicates Him before the world: three days in the grave and a bodily rising, the sign of Jonah. In refusing the test He exposes the hardness of His opponents, and in warning His disciples He shepherds them toward discernment. He is the risen Lord whose empty tomb remains the sign for every generation that demands proof.
study.god: God is not at the beck and call of unbelief; He will not perform to satisfy hearts that have already closed their eyes to His works. Yet He is supremely generous with the sign that counts, raising His Son from the dead as the public vindication of His whole mission. He calls His people to read the times rightly, to prize His word above sensation, and to guard against teaching that quietly corrupts, trusting the provision He has already proven.

crossReferences.note: Jonah three days and three nights in the belly of the great fish, the sign Jesus claims.
The earlier sign demand, where Jesus ties Jonah's three days to His own.
A little leaven leavens the whole lump, the metaphor applied to influence within the church.
Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed, faith resting on testimony rather than spectacle.

study.oneStory: In the face of a rejection so deep that rival parties unite against Him, Jesus withholds the sign they demand and points to the only one that will settle the matter: His resurrection, the sign of Jonah. The one story drives toward the cross and the empty tomb, God's decisive vindication of His Son, while Jesus trains His people to discern truth and beware the leaven of teaching that would corrupt the faith once given.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus refuse the demand for a sign from heaven?

options.text: The demand came from hardened hearts that would not believe, and He pointed instead to the sign of Jonah.
options.note: Verses 1-4 show a testing, unbelieving demand met with the promise of the resurrection sign.

options.text: He was unwilling to perform miracles for the crowds.
options.note: Jesus did work many miracles publicly; the issue was the testing, unbelieving motive, not miracles as such.

options.text: He had no power to give them a sign from heaven.
options.note: He refused by choice, not lack of power; He promises the greatest sign, His resurrection.

options.text: He agreed to give them every sign they asked for.
options.note: He explicitly refused all but the sign of Jonah.

quiz.q: What is the sign of Jonah?

options.text: Jesus' death and resurrection, foreshadowed by Jonah's three days in the great fish.
options.note: Matthew 12:40 ties Jonah's three days to the Son of Man in the earth.

options.text: A warning that judgment is coming on the generation.
options.note: Judgment is in view, but the sign itself is centrally the resurrection after three days.

options.text: A promise of calm weather like a red evening sky.
options.note: The weather sayings are a contrast, not the sign; this confuses the two.

options.text: Another public miracle to satisfy the Pharisees.
options.note: Jesus refuses more on-demand miracles; the sign is His rising, not a spectacle for them.

quiz.q: What did Jesus mean by the leaven of the Pharisees and Sadducees?

options.text: Their teaching, which spreads and corrupts quietly like yeast through dough.
options.note: Verse 12 says the disciples understood He meant their teaching.

options.text: A general warning to be careful around religious leaders.
options.note: It does warn about the leaders, but specifically about their doctrine, which this leaves vague.

options.text: A reminder to bring enough literal bread for the journey.
options.note: That is exactly the misunderstanding Jesus corrects; He was not speaking of bread.

options.text: An instruction to avoid all bread made with yeast.
options.note: The leaven is a metaphor for teaching, not a dietary rule.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus call the disciples of little faith?

options.text: They worried about lacking bread, forgetting that He had already fed thousands.
options.note: Verses 8-11 rebuke their anxiety in light of the feedings they had witnessed.

options.text: They failed to understand a difficult metaphor at first.
options.note: They did misunderstand, but the rebuke targets their forgetful anxiety, not mere slowness alone.
They still followed Him; the rebuke is for weak faith, not desertion.

options.text: They refused to believe He was the Messiah.
options.note: In the next scene Peter confesses Him as the Christ; the issue here is anxiety, not disbelief in His identity.

questions: The leaders wanted proof before they would believe. Where do you find yourself demanding a sign instead of trusting the word and works God has already given?
Leaven spreads quietly. What subtle ideas or influences are working their way into your thinking, and how can you test them against Scripture?
The disciples forgot the loaves they had seen multiplied. What past provision of God are you forgetting when present anxiety takes over?
The resurrection is the sign for every generation. How does the reality of the empty tomb steady you when you are tempted to doubt?
Jesus asks His people to read the signs of the times. Where do you need discernment right now to tell truth from counterfeit?

translationNotes.note: Several early manuscripts do not include the saying about the red evening and morning sky; it is retained here as in the BSB, and its point, discerning the times, matches Jesus' rebuke.
A wicked and adulterous generation uses adultery as a picture of covenant unfaithfulness to God, not only marital sin; the generation is unfaithful to the Lord who sent His Son.
The narrator supplies the interpretation: the leaven is the teaching of the Pharisees and Sadducees, confirming that the warning concerns doctrine, not literal bread.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Matthew 16:1-12. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment values in order; heading, line-break, and note markers were skipped. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
